also might might cause issues since currently importanize does not support comments outside of an import statement.

for example:

```python
import foo
# comment
import bar
```

when you run importanize, the following will be produced:

```python
import bar
import foo


# comment
```

so the safest thing to do is to include the comments with the appropriate imports:

```python
import bar
import foo  # comment
```

@iancoffey should this function also update the value of `/deis/platform/version` in etcd?  Otherwise, won't the same versions of each component just come right back up (despite a newer version of deisctl having been used to do so)?

Referencing the inner-workings of the `get_image` function:

```
#!/bin/bash
# usage: get_image <component_path>
IMAGE=`etcdctl get $1/image 2>/dev/null`

# if no image was set in etcd, we use the default plus the release string
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  RELEASE=`etcdctl get /deis/platform/version 2>/dev/null`

  # if no release was set in etcd, use the default provisioned with the server
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    source /etc/deis-release
    RELEASE=$DEIS_RELEASE
  fi

  IMAGE=$1:$RELEASE
fi

# remove leading slash
echo ${IMAGE#/}
```

Come to think of it, it needs to be noted that the upgrade won't upgrade any components for which a custom image has already been specified via etcd key `/deis/<component>/image`

The correct fix for "input shape 3 and I want concat by axis 2" is to insert permute layers. That is, input shape 3 is internally interpreted as (C,H,W) and axis=2 means the concat is on the 'W' axis. Since this is not supported by default, input should be first permuted to (W,H,C) and then do concat and then permute back. 

So the converter should be translating this concat layer to "permute-concat-permute". 

You want to try putting that fix in this PR? Its similar to how flatten in handled in "convert_flatten" function.

Sorry for not chiming in often on stuff like this recently, I've been slammed with design work on an entirely different system.

My biggest problem with this change is that you are changing the behavior of a well established mechanism for what amounts to an implementation detail. The implementation should achieve the design, not the other way around. Your implementation might only scan one channel at a time, but that is not a limitation that Spinel puts on the energy scan feature you are using. But keep reading.

A while back when we were talking about this I voiced the thought that the energy scan behavior should live in the host, which seems to be what you are trying to enable here. The difference was that I was imagining setting the channel and then repeatedly sampling RSSI, much like how an MCU might try to do an energy scan on a dumb transceiver IC.

However, in that case you can poll RSSI and get a response in a single SPI transaction, making it fast. With Spinel, you aren't going to have that sort of quick turn-around, so I admit that particular idea was not the greatest. There is a need for some sort of NCP-side helper for energy scans. I can also see why the existing energy scan mechanism might seem a bit clunky under those circumstances.

Ultimately, if you are going to use the existing energy scan mechanism, you need to adhere to the established behavior of that mechanism. This means that you would perform a scan on all of the given channels and return the results asynchronously, finishing by changing the property `PROP_MAC_SCAN_STATE` to `SCAN_STATE_IDLE`.

BUT... I think likely the best option would be to define a new property called `PROP_PHY_MAX_RSSI` (open to suggestions), which uses `PROP_PHY_CHAN` for the channel and `PROP_MAC_SCAN_PERIOD` for the duration. You query this property and you get back a result with the same transaction ID, making it much more convenient to use.

Alternatively, another option would be to fail if there was more than one channel present in the channel mask. But you'd still need to emit `PROP_MAC_SCAN_STATE` as `SCAN_STATE_IDLE` after you are done (which you aren't doing here).

-------------

Below are some other thoughts from earlier drafts of replying to this issue, included because I figured there might be some useful thoughts in there somewhere:

> It seemed inefficient to separate out a single function call into multiple individual commands just to pass the arguments. The Host is then required to make several async function calls (to send commands) and wait for their results in series.

Spinel is designed with the following philosophy:

* If you can reasonably implement a behavior with multiple properties, prefer that over implementing new commands. (There are cases where using properties would be a lot more convoluted than just using commands, such as the [host buffer offload](https://rawgit.com/openthread/openthread/master/doc/draft-spinel-protocol.html#rfc.section.9) feature))
* Avoid using structures for properties which aren't lists---such structures should instead be broken into multiple properties. 

These are intentional parts of Spinel's design which help ensure backward compatibility. Otherwise we shouldn't be using properties, we should be using commands.

Granted, what is "convoluted" and what is "reasonable" can be a matter of opinion, in which case we can discuss the matter as we are doing here.

> Besides scans, I couldn't find another OpenThread API that split it's calling logic between multiple function calls. For instance, all the _STREAM commands take all parameters inline.

The `*_STREAM` *properties* (they are not commands) are structures because we don't have any other way to ensure all of the individual attributes are associated together as one atomic unit.

Yes, I actually opted to cache a little more aggressively than the projector plugin - the whole result of `index_impl()` is cached in `self._index_cached`, which has the benefit that `tags_impl()` can also return without blocking.  However, unlike `is_active()` we don't want `tags_impl()` to stop refreshing the cache once it's gotten a positive result, because even a non-empty index (one that corresponds to an active plugin) may not be complete if more text summary data is still being written out to the event files.  So `tags_impl()` needs to keep launching that background thread to refresh the cache as long as it's still being invoked itself.

Once we're at that point, we could either decide to separately cache the `is_active()` state permanently once it's determined to be True.  This would have the benefit that `is_active()` would be even cheaper once it's true.  However, I figured that it was slightly more complex to have two different pieces of cached data, and that we might as well just keep `is_active()` simple and have it reflect whatever the current active state is based on the last refresh of `_index_cached` - after all, if the plugin really doesn't ever go inactive after being active, the end result is the same.

Note that I do make it so that `is_active()` itself won't launch a new thread if the current state is active.  It's only via `tags_impl()` that a new thread will be kicked off unconditionally; `is_active()` will take advantage of fresher data if it's present but won't actually refresh the data itself once the plugin is deemed active.

As of right now, all of these statements hold true:

```python
Organization().search() == Organization().search(fields=set())
Organization().search() == Organization().search(set())
Organization().search() == org.search(fields=set())
Organization().search() == org.search(set())
```

I've done this in order to make `search()` similar to `update()`. `update()` has this signature:

```python
def update(self, fields=None):
```

By default, `update()` sends all fields:

```python
org = entities.Organization(id=1, description='new description').update()
```

But you can also choose which fields to send:

```python
org = entities.Organization(id=1).read()
org.description = 'new description'
org = org.update(['description'])
```

Similarly, `search()` uses all fields:

```python
orgs = entities.Organization(description='new description').search()
```

But you may want to choose which fields to search by:

```python
org = entities.Organization(id=1).read()
orgs = org.search({'name'})
assert org.name == orgs[0].name
```

Now, you absolutely are right in pointing out that it's unusual to ask for a `set()` as an argument. That's pretty new.

I'm not sure that does what you think it does. That method is for appending LLIL to an existing function; it doesn't return an arbitrary LLIL instruction. In particular, it generates Lifted IL, not Low Level IL, and it appends it to the end of the `LowLevelILFunction` object passed to it, with no context. Here's an example, using that on an LLIL instruction `if (al != 0) then 12 @ 0x14000210 else 18 @ 0x140002105`:
```>>> a = LowLevelILFunction(Architecture['x86_64'])
>>> Architecture['x86_64'].get_instruction_low_level_il(bv.read(0x140002103, 8), 0x140002103, a)
2L
>>> a[0]
<il: if (!=) then 1 else 2>
>>> a[1]
<il: jump(0x140002110)>
>>> a[2]
<il: jump(0x140002105)>
```
Notice those are `LLIL_JUMP` instructions, rather than `LLIL_GOTO` instructions; the former are used when an instruction isn't in the current `LowLevelILFunction`, whereas the former is used when the target is in the current `LowLevelILFunction`. I'm not sure what the performance effects are on doing something like this, but it would probably be a little confusing, and you're also going to then have to track flags explicitly, since Lifted IL hasn't abstracted the flags away or folded conditions like LLIL does

Currently, as soon as a field in invalid, the submit button is disabled. So if the first field is focused, and then the submit button is clicked, the blur event is fired first and as a result, the submit button becomes disabled so a click event is not emitted and the form is not submitted. By delaying the blur, we can trigger the click event and submit the form before the button becomes disabled.

The normal way to infer `Aperture.pupil_plane_scale` -- when generating the Aperture from an image and not geometric parameters -- is to look for the illuminated pixel farthest from the image center and declare that distance to be the radius.  You then divide by (half) the specified diameter in meters to get the scale.

Unfortunately, this is only accurate to ~1 pixel (see comment [here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/%23549/galsim/phase_psf.py#L281)), since we can't know exactly where inside the pixel in question the edge of the pupil really is.  So _pupil_plane_scale lets you override this calculation if you have a more accurate value.  Actually, "more accurate" is probably not the right characterization, since the error is an inevitable consequence of discretizing the pupil into an array.  Really, the override is there so that its possible to create an `Aperture` parametrically, write the `illuminated` attribute to disk, and then recreate a byte-identical `Aperture` from the recorded image in this unit test.  I puzzled over how best to accomplish this for quite a while a few weeks ago.  If you have a better solution, I'm certainly open to it!
